summary: Although I never lived in Nashville, my mother was from there, and we spent a lot of time visiting during the summers because her mother and grandmother lived there. I have a vivid memory from the Nashville train station when I was about four or five, walking through the white section with my mother and witnessing a policeman confront her with a racial slur. Nashville was also where I experienced some childhood activities like Bible school, which I disliked, and visiting my grandmother’s large house with its goldfish pond and lawn.

I spent the first five years of my life in Fort Valley, Georgia, a small town near Macon. It was home to Fort Valley State College, where my father was the president, so I grew up on the college campus. My early childhood memories are rooted there before we moved to Pennsylvania when I was five.

Macon, Georgia, was near Fort Valley where I lived as a young child. Although I didn’t spend much time there directly, it was the larger city close to Fort Valley State College where my father was president. My early years were shaped by this region before relocating to Pennsylvania.

Fort Valley State College was where my father served as president during my early childhood in Georgia. Growing up on the campus gave me a unique, protected environment, and it was a significant part of my upbringing before we moved to Pennsylvania. The college was a central part of our family life and community.

American University is similar in size to the college campus where I grew up, and it helps me describe the small, close-knit world I inhabited as a child. My father was president of a college with a campus not much bigger than American University’s, and I had the freedom to roam the gym, dormitories, and other facilities during summers. It was a sheltered, friendly environment that shaped my early experiences.

Lincoln University in Pennsylvania was where my father was president after we moved from Georgia. It was a prominent historically black college that attracted many famous black figures like Walter White and Paul Robeson, who visited and spoke on campus. Growing up next door to the church and auditorium where these events took place gave me a unique exposure to civil rights leaders and cultural icons during the 1940s and 1950s.

I remember Walter White visiting Lincoln University when I was a child. He was the head of the NAACP for many years and was very fair-skinned, almost looking white, but I knew he was black. When he arrived, he came in a big shiny black car escorted by Pennsylvania state troopers, which made a strong impression on me as a kid.

The NAACP was represented on our campus by its leader Walter White, who visited Lincoln University while I was growing up. The organization’s presence was significant in the black community and civil rights movement, and seeing its head arrive with such ceremony made me aware of its importance. The NAACP was part of the broader world of black leadership that influenced my upbringing.

Paul Robeson was one of the most memorable figures I met as a child at Lincoln University. I have a picture of me sitting on his lap while he sang a Russian folk song called 'Four Insurgent Generals.' His deep voice resonated with me, and after his performances, there were receptions at our house where I would run around with my siblings and neighbors. Meeting Robeson was a fabulous experience that left a lasting impression.

I have a childhood photograph of myself with W. E. B. Du Bois taken when we lived in Fort Valley, Georgia. Du Bois was one of the prominent black leaders who visited the college campus where my father was president. His presence, along with other notable figures, helped expose me early to important personalities in black America.

My parents told me that I met Albert Einstein, although I have no personal memory of the encounter. This meeting was part of the many distinguished visitors who came through the college campus where my father was president. It symbolizes the broad and sophisticated world my parents introduced me to during my childhood.

Attending the George School, a Quaker school near Philadelphia, introduced me to Quaker beliefs and the principle of nonviolence. This was a significant contrast to the segregated public schools I had attended before. The Quaker emphasis on pacifism influenced my early views and later shaped my approach during the civil rights movement.

The George School was a co-ed Quaker prep school I attended after my local segregated schools closed. It was a small, intimate school that provided a far superior education to what I would have received locally. The school exposed me to Quaker values, including pacifism, which later influenced my participation in the civil rights movement.

entity: Lower Oxford Township Consolidated High School
summary: Before attending the George School, I went to the Lower Oxford Township Consolidated High School in Pennsylvania, which was a small, under-resourced school in a segregated town. My parents were not satisfied with the quality of education there, which is why they sought better opportunities for me. The school experience was quite limited compared to what I later received at the George School.

Oxford, Pennsylvania, was the nearby town to where I lived while attending Lincoln University. It was a segregated town, and I remember incidents where Lincoln students challenged segregation by sitting downstairs in the movie theater instead of the Jim Crow gallery. Although I rarely went into town, Oxford represented the segregated society outside our relatively protected campus environment.

We moved to Pennsylvania when I was five because my father became president of Lincoln University. Living in Pennsylvania exposed me to a different environment from Georgia, including segregated schools and towns like Oxford. Despite segregation, I had a relatively protected childhood on the college campus and attended schools that shaped my education and views, including the Quaker George School.
